request.question: Elton John's songwriting partner for the song "Indian Sunset" was born in what year?

title: Come Down in Time
Elton John (album)
Ghetto Gospel
Ray Cooper
Elton John: Me, Myself & I
The Goaldiggers Song
Bernie Taupin
Elton John
Indian Sunset
I Don't Wanna Go on with You Like That
sentences: "Come Down in Time" is the second track on Elton John's third album, "Tumbleweed Connection", released in 1970.
The lyric was written by Bernie Taupin, Elton's long time writing partner.
The song was originally recorded for John's second album, "Elton John".
The song was played alongside the Sydney Orchestra in a 1986 live performance.
Elton John is the second studio album by English singer-songwriter Elton John, released on 10 April 1970 by DJM Records.
It was released by Uni Records as John's debut album in the United States; many people there assumed it was his first album, as "Empty Sky" was not released in that country until 1975.
"Elton John" includes his breakthrough single "Your Song", and helped to establish his career during what was considered the "singer-songwriter" era of popular music.
In the US, it was certified gold in February 1971 by the Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA).
In the same year, it was nominated for the Grammy Award for Album of the Year.
"Ghetto Gospel" is a song by rapper Tupac Shakur, which was originally released as the second single on his posthumous 2004 album "Loyal to the Game".
It samples Elton John's 1971 track, "Indian Sunset".
The single topped the charts in the United Kingdom (for 3 weeks), Australia, Czech Republic and the Republic of Ireland.
Raymond Thomas "Ray" Cooper (born 19 September 1947) is an English virtuoso percussionist.

He is a session and road-tour percussionist, and occasional actor, who has worked with several musically diverse bands and artists including George Harrison, Billy Joel, Rick Wakeman, Eric Clapton, Pink Floyd and Elton John.
Cooper absorbed the influence of rock drummers from the 1960s and 1970s such as Ginger Baker, Carmine Appice, and John Bonham.
Incorporation of unusual instruments (for rock drummers of the time) such as cowbells, glockenspiel, and tubular bells, along with several standard kit elements, helped create a highly varied setup.

Continually modified to this day, Cooper's percussion set offers an enormous array of percussion instruments for sonic diversity such as the tambourine, congas, crash cymbals, cowbells, roto toms, tubular bells, the gong, snare and timpani.
For two decades Cooper honed his technique; In the 1990s, he reinvented his style.

He is known for the 7-minute percussion and drum solos he performed during the years 1990–1991 for Eric Clapton and for the 7-minute percussion and drum solos during all the 1994 Face to Face Tours with Billy Joel, and Elton John, and the tours with the Elton John band during the years of 1994–1995.
Elton John: Me, Myself & I is a 2007 documentary filmed after the death of Elton John's good friend Diana and other soul shaking events that caused him to reassess his life.
It is a candid appraisal by Elton John (tongue in cheek) of his fame, drug use, sexuality, and mistakenly taking his life for granted.
It was filmed in Las Vegas, Nevada, United States
"The Goaldiggers Song" is a charity single by Elton John, released in 1977.
The song is written for the Goaldigger's charity to provide playing fields in under-privileged areas.
At the time in 1977, Elton John was living reclused in London; the only time he would go out was to go see his football team Watford F.C. play.
The single never charted anywhere, as it was a limited charity release-only 500 copies were pressed, approximately half of which were signed by Elton.
It was available for a cost of five UK pounds by mail-order only and featured Elton and a number of other British celebrities including Jimmy Hill and Eric Morecombe in conversation on the B-side.
The tapes were destroyed after the pressing, so no other official copies of the recording exist.

Bernard John Taupin (born 22 May 1950) is an English lyricist, poet, and singer, best known for his long-term collaboration with Elton John, writing the lyrics for the majority of the star's songs, and making his lyrics some of the best known in music history.
Sir Elton Hercules John, (born Reginald Kenneth Dwight; 25 March 1947), is an English singer, pianist, and composer.
He has worked with lyricist Bernie Taupin as his songwriting partner since 1967; they have collaborated on more than 30 albums to date.
In his five-decade career Elton John has sold more than 300million records, making him one of the best-selling music artists in the world.
He has more than fifty Top 40 hits, including seven consecutive No. 1 US albums, 58 "Billboard" Top 40 singles, 27 Top 10, four No. 2 and nine No. 1.
For 31 consecutive years (1970–2000) he had at least one song in the "Billboard" Hot 100.

His tribute single, re-penned in dedication to the late Princess Diana, "Candle in the Wind 1997" sold over 33million copies worldwide and is the best-selling single in the history of the UK and US singles charts.
He has also composed music, produced records, and has occasionally acted in films.
John owned Watford Football Club from 1976 to 1987, and 1997 to 2002.
He is an honorary Life President of the club, and in 2014 had a stand named after him at the club's home stadium.
"Indian Sunset" is a 1971 song, written and recorded by Elton John, with lyrics by his long-time songwriting partner, Bernie Taupin.
It was from John's album "Madman Across the Water".
The song chronicles the story of an unnamed American Indian warrior on the verge of defeat from the white man.
Taupin was inspired to write the lyrics after visiting a Native American reservation.
"I Don't Wanna Go on with You Like That" is a hit song from British performer Elton John's 1988 album, "Reg Strikes Back".
The song was written by John and his long-time songwriting partner, Bernie Taupin.
The song reached #30 on the UK Singles Chart.

It fared better in the United States, peaking at #2 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 chart in August 1988, becoming John's biggest hit of the 1980s there (kept out of the top spot by fellow British singer and vocal collaborator George Michael's hit single "Monkey").
The song also became John's eighth #1 on the adult contemporary chart.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: Elton John's songwriting partner for the song "Indian Sunset" was born in what year?
hop1_docs: Indian Sunset | "Indian Sunset" is a 1971 song, written and recorded by Elton John, with lyrics by his long-time songwriting partner, Bernie Taupin.  It was from John's album "Madman Across the Water".  The song chronicles the story of an unnamed American Indian warrior on the verge of defeat from the white man.  Taupin was inspired to write the lyrics after visiting a Native American reservation.

Elton John (album) | Elton John is the second studio album by English singer-songwriter Elton John, released on 10 April 1970 by DJM Records.  It was released by Uni Records as John's debut album in the United States; many people there assumed it was his first album, as "Empty Sky" was not released in that country until 1975.  "Elton John" includes his breakthrough single "Your Song", and helped to establish his career during what was considered the "singer-songwriter" era of popular music.  In the US, it was certified gold in February 1971 by the Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA).  In the same year, it was nominated for the Grammy Award for Album of the Year.

Come Down in Time | "Come Down in Time" is the second track on Elton John's third album, "Tumbleweed Connection", released in 1970.  The lyric was written by Bernie Taupin, Elton's long time writing partner.  The song was originally recorded for John's second album, "Elton John".  The song was played alongside the Sydney Orchestra in a 1986 live performance.

Ghetto Gospel | "Ghetto Gospel" is a song by rapper Tupac Shakur, which was originally released as the second single on his posthumous 2004 album "Loyal to the Game".  It samples Elton John's 1971 track, "Indian Sunset".  The single topped the charts in the United Kingdom (for 3 weeks), Australia, Czech Republic and the Republic of Ireland.

Love Songs (Elton John album) | Love Songs is a compilation album by British singer-songwriter Elton John.  The album was first released on 6 November 1995 by John's own label The Rocket Record Company, in conjunction with Mercury Records.  The album was released in North America by MCA Records almost a year after the European release, on 24 September 1996.  In the US, it was certified gold in December 1996, platinum in March 1997, 2× platinum in December 1998 and 3× platinum in August 2000 by the RIAA.

Elton John's Christmas Party | Elton John's Christmas Party is a Christmas-themed compilation album from British singer-songwriter Elton John.  It features his 1973 Christmas single, "Step into Christmas", and a new duet with Joss Stone, "Calling It Christmas".  According to John's introduction in the liner notes, as opposed to doing an album of his versions of already famous songs, he chose to do a compilation with some of his favorite holiday songs by other artists.

I Don't Wanna Go on with You Like That | "I Don't Wanna Go on with You Like That" is a hit song from British performer Elton John's 1988 album, "Reg Strikes Back".  The song was written by John and his long-time songwriting partner, Bernie Taupin.  The song reached #30 on the UK Singles Chart.  It fared better in the United States, peaking at #2 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 chart in August 1988, becoming John's biggest hit of the 1980s there (kept out of the top spot by fellow British singer and vocal collaborator George Michael's hit single "Monkey").  The song also became John's eighth #1 on the adult contemporary chart.
hop1_titles: I Don't Wanna Go on with You Like That
response.summary_1: First-hop evidence: passage [1] credits "Indian Sunset" as having "lyrics by his long-time songwriting partner, Bernie Taupin." Bridge entity: Bernie Taupin. Missing fact: Bernie Taupin's year of birth is not stated in the provided passages. Retrieval directions: 1) Open Bernie Taupin's biography (e.g., his Wikipedia page or an authoritative music biography) and check the lead/infobox for "born" to obtain his birth year, or run the query: "Bernie Taupin born OR 'born in' site:wikipedia.org". No identity ambiguity in the passages.
hop2_docs: Wikidumper.org | WikiDumper.org is a website created by Clifford A. Pickover that promises to permanently record a snapshot of the "best of the Wikipedia rejects", articles that are slated for deletion at the site.  WikiDumper was launched on November 4, 2006, and accepts user submissions.  Although the site doesn't specify its criteria for inclusion, many of its articles don't cite their sources.  The site has been criticized as likely to be less accurate than Wikipedia.
Two Rooms: Celebrating the Songs of Elton John & Bernie Taupin | Two Rooms: Celebrating the Songs of Elton John & Bernie Taupin

Tribe (Bernie Taupin album) | Tribe is a 1987 solo album by Bernie Taupin issued by RCA Records.  The album includes the two singles, "Friend of the Flag" and "Citizen Jane".  Music videos were made for both singles.

Paca Thomas | Paca Thomas is the owner of pacaworks.com, a multi-media production company.  He can also be heard weekly as Bernie Taupin's sidekick/producer on Bernie Taupin's American Roots Radio on SiriusXM's The Loft.  He is the son of Philadelphia DJ George Thomas.

List of songs with lyrics by Bernie Taupin | Bernie Taupin is a lyricist, perhaps most notable for writing many of the lyrics to the music of Elton John.  Over the years, he has written songs for a variety of other artists including Alice Cooper, Heart, Melissa Manchester, Starship, Rod Stewart and Richie Sambora.

Maxine Feibelman | Maxine Phyllis Feibelman (born 1952) is a former seamstress for the Elton John band who was married for several years to John's frequent collaborator Bernie Taupin.  Her romance, marriage, and break-up with Taupin influenced at least eight of Taupin's and John's songs, she provided the title and refrain for one of their songs, and she supported John's path toward his distinct stage costume style.

Bernie Taupin | Bernard John Taupin (born 22 May 1950) is an English lyricist, poet, and singer, best known for his long-term collaboration with Elton John, writing the lyrics for the majority of the star's songs, and making his lyrics some of the best known in music history.
hop2_titles: Two Rooms: Celebrating the Songs of Elton John & Bernie Taupin
List of songs with lyrics by Bernie Taupin
response.summary_2: Elton John's songwriting partner Bernie Taupin was born in 1950. His biography lists his birthdate as 22 May 1950.
